full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|5.00|> As we do most every year,<|7.64|> <|7.64|> several of us on staff went to Union Station<|12.10|> <|12.10|> to impose ashes on Ash Wednesday.<|15.40|> <|15.40|> It's called Ashes to Go.<|17.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.28|> Deacon Jack and I were standing together and talking between folks coming up to us,<|6.58|> <|7.00|> and we were discussing the upcoming season of Lent and the concept of giving things up.<|15.08|> <|16.52|> Jack talked of fasting, fasting from things that harden our hearts.<|23.80|> <|24.02|> He spoke specifically of fasting from handouts.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This was not a long conversation, it was a gentle musing as we drank coffee, warmed our<|8.28|> <|8.28|> very cold hands on the warm cups, and waited for the next batch of commuters to disembark.<|16.02|> <|16.78|> We quickly moved on to imposing ashes and other topics.<|21.38|> <|21.38|> We were in the midst of the night.<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A dear and earnest parishioner asked for an appointment the very next day.<|7.24|> <|8.00|> They were deeply, deeply distraught by the state of the world,<|13.70|> <|14.86|> the things happening to people they love,<|17.30|> <|18.06|> and they were deeply disturbed by how hard their heart had become through it all.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They felt helpless against the onslaught of the 24-7 news cycle and troubled, deeply troubled, by how viscerally they felt rage about all of it.<|16.70|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My mind immediately went back to that conversation the previous chilly morning on that city sidewalk and the comment from our beloved Deacon Jack about a fast from hate.<|16.48|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A fast from hate. Not chocolate or wine or coffee, which I could not do,<|7.20|> <|8.64|> and no one wants me to give up coffee. Or a fast from social media, but a fast from hate.<|17.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> from that emotion that can insidiously sneak in like a cunning fox<|8.16|> <|8.16|> and steal our peace of mind, our sense of self, our ability to feel compassion.<|17.70|> <|19.32|> Succumbing to hate can render us unrecognizable even to ourselves.<|25.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.74|> So I shared this with the distressed parishioner, this idea of a fast from hate, and the notion<|10.30|> <|10.30|> immediately resonated with them.<|12.62|> <|13.48|> So we found a few prayers and a psalm that brought specific hope in the midst of a heart<|22.40|> <|22.40|> on its way to hardening.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And there was such relief in their voice, such hope that they could have agency with God's help<|9.96|> <|9.96|> in this battle against the damage that what was outside of them was causing inside of them.<|20.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.70|> This Lenten fast from hate is to be their 40-day journey<|6.70|> <|6.86|> of reclaiming themselves, their dignity, their sanity,<|11.86|> <|13.28|> and building up the resilience<|16.76|> <|16.76|> to endure what seems to be a never-ending barrage<|21.76|> <|23.90|> these days.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And all of this came up again for me as I was studying the gospel lesson for this evening,<|9.86|> <|9.86|> this passage from Luke where the Pharisees come to Jesus with the news that Herod, who<|16.28|> <|16.28|> has already killed John the Baptist, wants to kill him.<|21.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I love this little, it's such a short passage we get, just this little bit of text for so many reasons.<|8.00|> <|9.00|> I love the image of Jesus as a mother hen gathering chicks under the wings.<|19.36|> <|20.64|> I love Jesus' colorful calling Herod a fox.<|25.70|> <|25.70|> I love Jesus' colorful calling Herod a fox.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus' clear and deep devotion for Jerusalem.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> But also, I love it for this depiction of the Pharisees.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> The Pharisees are often disparaged in our thinking about Scripture and in older biblical commentary.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> At best, they are viewed as a foil for Jesus and often a stumbling block.<|7.38|> <|8.88|> My friends, historically, Lent was a very dangerous time to be Jewish.<|17.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Our readings throughout this season refer to the Jews and lump together all sorts, all<|11.28|> <|11.28|> groups of Jewish folk.<|15.10|> <|15.10|> And it seems that in later centuries, Christians easily forgot that Jesus himself and many of<|22.88|> <|22.88|> his followers were Jewish.<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Anti-Semitic sentiment and activity would always spike during Lent.<|7.96|> <|9.08|> In fact, I heard a quote in an interview this week that really hit home with this notion.<|15.36|> <|16.72|> Anti-Semitism is a light sleeper.<|20.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So I appreciate that the Pharisees in this text are very clearly not the bad guys.<|10.32|> <|11.12|> They are so clearly and positively portrayed.<|15.30|> <|15.94|> They are coming to warn Jesus that Herod is after him.<|21.04|> <|21.62|> They encourage him to move along to stay safe.<|25.82|> <|25.82|> Amen.<|26.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There are some scholars in more recent time who argue that Jesus himself was more closely aligned with the Pharisees than any other Jewish group in their observance of the law, Sabbath.<|17.78|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> tradition, in their curiosity, in their willingness to engage with Jesus in discussion and debate<|8.86|> <|8.86|> about his teachings and actions. We see Jesus eat in the Gospels again and again with the Pharisees<|18.42|> <|18.42|> and enter the homes of Pharisees.<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If they hated or opposed Jesus, these Pharisees, why warn him to flee?<|7.90|> <|8.34|> Why not just have him stay right there and let Herod find him?<|11.54|> <|13.02|> One commentator's take on this story strikes me as both humorous and deeply insightful.<|20.06|> <|21.20|> Tell me if you agree.<|22.34|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He said, "Customary interpretation has made all of the Pharisees into rigid hypocrites.<|12.72|> <|12.72|> Some probably were.<|15.22|> <|15.22|> Find me a crowd of 100 people, pastors, professors, postal workers, anyone, and I will find you<|23.74|> <|23.74|> at least 15 people in that crowd who are rigid hypocrites."<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That is funny.<|2.06|> <|3.20|> Amen?<|3.96|> <|6.82|> Of course not in here, in this crowd of 100.<|9.72|> <|10.28|> Yeah, no.<|10.98|> <|12.38|> So if we read this closely, if we pay attention to the text,<|17.02|> <|17.64|> we cannot walk away with an idea that these Pharisees were Jesus' enemies.<|25.34|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Pharisees love Jerusalem as much as Jesus does.<|4.76|> <|5.78|> It is the center of their world too.<|8.18|> <|9.14|> It is the end point of their festival pilgrimages.<|13.04|> <|13.56|> It is the Holy of Holies.<|15.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So, you see, my friends, this is actually a really complicated little story, made more<|7.30|> <|7.30|> so when we discard the easy idea of who is good and who is bad, made more so when we<|16.62|> <|16.62|> are curious and open to looking under the proverbial hood.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Aren't most stories and situations in our lives more complicated than they look on the surface?<|9.26|> <|10.84|> It is far too easy to dualize and characterize both in Scripture and in our lives.<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> if you've been awake through any of my recent sermons and it's okay if you haven't but I will<|5.94|> <|5.94|> tell you this is a drum I have been beating again and again and again because it keeps bubbling up<|12.00|> <|12.00|> in the gospels for me and it is so relevant to the time in which we are living<|17.58|> <|17.58|> as relevant as it has ever been<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is far too easy to characterize and dualize, both in scripture and our lives.<|9.76|> <|10.56|> Good, evil, in, out, love, hate, right, wrong.<|16.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.12|> Eilif Professor Eric Smith, who's led many dean's forums here at St. John's,<|7.26|> <|7.98|> wants us to encounter the Pharisees here, remembering that Herod was a puppet of Pontius Pilate<|17.50|> <|17.50|> and therefore of Rome.<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As we see in this encounter, it is not the Pharisees who wanted Jesus killed, but rather<|8.06|> <|8.06|> the non-Jewish ruling powers.<|12.04|> <|14.18|> So what of this story during Lent?<|18.68|> <|19.40|> What of the church's call to observe a holy Lent in part through fasting?<|25.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This text from Luke reminds me to fast from jumping to conclusions, to fast from being incurious, to fast from reductive thinking that makes things far simpler than they truly are.<|23.06|> <|24.36|> To fast from hate.<|27.42|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.68|><|endoftext|>
